coredata.dc:title: Swift heavy-ion irradiation of graphene oxide: Localized reduction and formation of sp-hybridized carbon chains
coredata.dc:creator: Olejniczak, Andrzej
Nebogatikova, Nadezhda A.
Frolov, Aleksei V.
Kulik, Miroslaw
Antonova, Irina V.
Skuratov, Vladimir A.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report the fabrication of nanometer-sized reduced graphene oxide (rGO) spots by swift heavy-ion (SHI) bombardment. Such structures can be considered graphene quantum dots (QDs) embedded in a non-conducting matrix. Both the number density and the diameter of the rGO spots can be tailored by a suitable choice of irradiation parameters (i.e., ion type, fluence, and energy). The degree of graphene oxide defunctionalization by SHIs with different energies scaled well with the deposited electronic energy density. The resistance of the samples decreased nonlinearly with increasing ion dose and, at fluences above 1013 ions/cm2, was orders of magnitude lower than the initial value. An increase in the electronic stopping power of the ion resulted (i) in suppression of the structural ordering at low fluences and (ii) in increased amorphization efficiency and formation of sp-hybridized carbon chains of both polyynes and polycumulenes at high fluences. A hypothesis suggesting that the sp-C chains are bridges joining opposite sides of nanoholes created inside the track core and thus assuming the formation of a coupled QD-antidot system is presented. These phenomena were found to be absent in comparative experiments with 200 keV Xe ion irradiation, i.e., in the nuclear stopping regime.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effects of ion irradiation in the electronic stopping regime
The effects of ion irradiation in the nuclear stopping regime

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Cooperation Program between Polish scientific institutions

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to Dr. N. E. Lizunov (FLNR, JINR) for performing the SEM experiments and to Dr. A. P. Orlov (IRE RAS) for his helpful suggestions and assistance with setting up the temperature-dependent resistance measurements. This research was partially supported by the Cooperation Program between Polish scientific institutions and JINR within Theme No. 04-5-1131-2017/2021 . NAN gratefully acknowledges financial support by the Russian Foundation for Basic Research (RFBR, No. 18-32-00449 ) and the Russian President's scholarship , project No. SP-5416.2018.2 . AVF (themperature-dependent resistance measurements) acknowledges support by the Russian Science Foundation (project No. 16-12-10411 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Changes in the surface atomic fraction of C atoms for GO films irradiated with different ions as functions of the ion fluence (a) and deposited electronic energy density (b). The curves have been fitted according to the direct impact model. Variations in the Raman-derived spectral parameters for GO as functions of ion fluence (c) and deposited electronic energy density (d). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

AFM height (a, top) and lateral force (a, bottom) images of GO specimens: virgin and 107 MeV Kr ion-irradiated to fluences of 2
⋅
1011 cm− 2 and 3
⋅
1013 cm−2. The image size is
400 × 400
nm2. The height scales (z) of the images are specified in each topography image. Flattened zoomed-in views of the yellow boxed regions (for a sample irradiated to a fluence of 2
⋅
1011 cm−2) showing two neighboring rGO spots, along with height and lateral force profiles, are shown in (b) and (c). An SEM image of 167 MeV Xe ion-irradiated GO and SE intensity signal profile along a rGO spot is shown in (d) and (e). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
SEM images of GO thin films: (a) unirradiated, (b) after 167 MeV Xe ion irradiation to a fluence of 1013 cm−2, and (c) after 200 keV Xe ion irradiation to a fluence of 1016 cm−2.

Electrical characterization of virgin and 46 MeV Ar ion-irradiated GO thin films: (a) I-V characteristics acquired at room temperature; (b) changes in the current recorded at room temperature in a lateral configuration at a voltage of 5 V as a function of ion fluence (the line is to guide the eye); (c) temperature dependences of the sheet resistance plotted in Arrhenius coordinates; note that at high temperatures, the R(T) dependencies deviate from the ES-VRH fits (red lines) and obey the Arrhenius law (green dashed lines); (d) dose dependence of the activation energy, calculated from the high-temperature region of R(T), and band gap, estimated from the ES-VRH model (the lines are to guide the eye). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Representative Raman spectrum of ion-irradiated GO (107 MeV Kr,
Φ =
10
13
cm−2) recorded with constant scanning of the laser beam over the sample surface (a). This special technique was applied to minimize depletion of sp-C signal. A proposed nanosized rGO structure with a sp-C chain formed from the zigzag edge by the removal of C atoms on both sides of the chain is shown in (b). Fully optimized, phenyl end-capped C8 polyyne chain (c). The vibrational frequencies (
ω
α
) were scaled by a factor of 0.964 [53]. Note that geometric constraint results in both a blue-shift of the LO mode and a decrease in the BLA value. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Studied ions, their energies and corresponding stopping power values calculated for graphite using the SRIM-2013 code.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a – values estimated for a single graphene sheet.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TA and ES-VRH fitting results for virgin and 46 MeV Ar ion-irradiated GO specimens.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: n.d. – not determined, the resistance at low temperatures exceeded the measurement range.

head.ce:title: Swift heavy-ion irradiation of graphene oxide: Localized reduction and formation of sp-hybridized carbon chains

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Nicolaus Copernicus University, ul.Gagarina 7, 87-100, Toruń, Poland

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Joliot-Curie 6, Dubna, 141980, Russia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Rzhanov Institute of Semiconductor Physics, SB RAS, Lavrent'ev av. 13, Novosibirsk, 630090, Russia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Novosibirsk State University, Novosibirsk, Pirogov str. 2, 630090, Russia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Kotel'nikov Institute of Radio Engineering and Electronics RAS

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Novosibirsk State Technical University, Novosibirsk, 630073, Russia

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physics, Maria Curie-Sklodowska University, pl. M. Curie Skłodowskiej 1, 20-031, Lublin, Poland

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Faculty of Chemistry, Nicolaus Copernicus University, ul.Gagarina 7, 87-100, Toruń, Poland.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report the fabrication of nanometer-sized reduced graphene oxide (rGO) spots by swift heavy-ion (SHI) bombardment. Such structures can be considered graphene quantum dots (QDs) embedded in a non-conducting matrix. Both the number density and the diameter of the rGO spots can be tailored by a suitable choice of irradiation parameters (i.e., ion type, fluence, and energy). The degree of graphene oxide defunctionalization by SHIs with different energies scaled well with the deposited electronic energy density. The resistance of the samples decreased nonlinearly with increasing ion dose and, at fluences above 1013 ions/cm2, was orders of magnitude lower than the initial value. An increase in the electronic stopping power of the ion resulted (i) in suppression of the structural ordering at low fluences and (ii) in increased amorphization efficiency and formation of sp-hybridized carbon chains of both polyynes and polycumulenes at high fluences. A hypothesis suggesting that the sp-C chains are bridges joining opposite sides of nanoholes created inside the track core and thus assuming the formation of a coupled QD-antidot system is presented. These phenomena were found to be absent in comparative experiments with 200 keV Xe ion irradiation, i.e., in the nuclear stopping regime.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, there has been enormous scientific progress in the field of graphene-related materials. In particular, graphene oxide (GO), prepared by introducing oxygen-containing groups and providing different levels of exfoliation to graphite crystals through chemical oxidation [1], is under consideration as a low-cost alternative for micro- and larger-scale applications. One of the most important transformations of GO is its reduction, which yields reduced graphene oxide (rGO). Due to its partially restored π-network, rGO has substantially improved electrical conductivity, making it an attractive material for modern electronic applications, especially those requiring large area coverage. GO, rGO, and their composites have found a wide range of applications including, for example, light-controlled conductive switching [2], the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants (e.g., rhodamine B) [3], and the fabrication of luminescent and water soluble QDs-GO nanocomposites for in vivo bioimaging [4].

rGO is typically obtained from chemical, thermal, or electrochemical treatments [1]. Considerable effort has also been devoted to reducing GO using different kinds of radiation, including radiation form the UV to the IR region [5–8], γ-ray irradiation processing in solution [9,10] and under a H2 atmosphere [11,12], and H2/Ar plasma treatment [13]. Surprisingly, little attention has been given to ion-irradiation processing of GO. To the best of our knowledge, only a few studies have investigated the transformations in GO upon heavy-ion bombardment in the keV [14–18] and [19] MeV energy range. In the former case, where nuclear energy loss dominates, the complete deoxygenation of GO was achieved at high doses and was accompanied by amorphization of the material and generation of nanocones and whirlpool-like structures on the surface. In the latter case [19], changes in the composition and structural properties of GO after irradiation with swift heavy ions (SHIs), i.e., in the electronic stopping regime, were investigated. Using various experimental techniques, the authors showed that the removal of oxygen-containing groups is accompanied by a decrease in the interlayer distance and an improvement in electrochemical performance (H2O2 sensing).

Because of the potential to precisely engineer conductive and insulating regions, localized reduction of GO is of particular interest from a nanoelectronics point of view. To date, GO nanopatterning has been archived by laser [20,21], focused ion [15,17] and electron [22,23] beams, as well as by various scanning probe microscopy techniques based on heated-tip [24], electrochemical [25–28], and catalytic [29] lithography. Examples of structures successfully fabricated using these methods include circuits, capacitors, transistors and sensors.

The aim of the present study was to undertake systematic investigations of the irradiation effects on GO over a broad range of ion energies and fluences. Given the ability of SHIs to displace large amounts of energy along a small cylindrical volume surrounding an ion trajectory, a highly localized structural modification inside so-called ‘tracks’, is possible for a variety of materials [30]. In this work, we intended to employ this unique feature of SHI irradiation to create nanometer-sized deoxygenated regions in GO films. Assuming that the localized reduction occurs along the ion trajectory through several GO sheets, the resulting structure can be considered an array of vertically arranged graphene quantum dots (QDs) embedded in a non-conducting matrix and, in our opinion, is unlikely to be easily obtained using conventional approaches. Another intriguing finding of the present study is the observation of sp-hybridized carbon chains in samples subjected to high-dose irradiation in the electronic stopping regime. A possible explanation for this phenomenon, i.e., that these carbon chains function as bridges, joining opposite sides of a nanohole created inside the track core and thus leading to formation of a coupled QD-antidot system, is discussed.

A solution of GO obtained from expandable graphite by the Hummers method was diluted with water to a final concentration of 0.2 mg/mL and then ultrasonicated to afford a higher content of monolayer flakes. The films were fabricated by vacuum filtration of the resulting suspension through a membrane filter, followed by extensive washing with Milli-Q quality deionized water, drying in air, and peeling of the film from the membrane. The procedure allowed us to prepare large-area uniform thin films.

Graphene oxide thin films were irradiated with 167 MeV Xe, 107 MeV Kr, and 46 MeV Ar ions at the IC-100 cyclotron (FLNR, JINR), the fluence ranges of which were 5
⋅
1010–1
⋅
1013 cm−2, 2
⋅
1011-3
⋅
1013 cm−2, and 6
⋅
1010-2
⋅
1013 cm−2, respectively. Low-energy (200 keV) Xe ion irradiation (fluence range of 1012-1016 cm−2) was performed using the UNIMAS-79 implanter (UMCS). Since it is challenging to experimentally obtain the stopping power values for GO and also its composition and density changes upon ion-irradiation, the stopping power values (keV/nm) were calculated for graphite and scaled by a factor of 0.333 to estimate the energy deposited in a single graphene sheet (Table 1 ).

The irradiation effects on GO films were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman spectroscopy, and electrical transport measurements. The surfaces of virgin and irradiated GO films were observed in secondary electron (SE) mode using a Hitachi SU8020 field emission scanning electron microscope operating at 3 keV. XPS measurements were performed with a K-Alpha (Thermo Scientific) spectrometer equipped with a monochromatized Al Kα X-ray source (1486.68 eV). The spectra were acquired at a 90° takeoff angle in the constant analyzer energy mode with pass energies of 100 eV (0.5 eV step) and 20 eV (0.05 eV step) for survey and high-resolution region scans, respectively. For poorly conductive samples, a dual-beam charge compensation system (flood gun) was used to minimize surface charging. The base pressure in the analysis chamber was 7
⋅
10−10 mbar, and during sample analysis with the flood gun on, it was in the range of (1-3)
⋅
10−7 mbar. AFM measurements were performed using an NT-MDT Ntegra scanning probe microscope operating in contact mode. Silicon cantilever tips (CSG01) with a typical resonance frequency of 9.8 kHz, a force constant of 0.03 N/m, and a tip radius less than 10 nm were used. Differentiation of regions according to their chemical composition was based on images recorded in lateral force (friction force) mode. The current-voltage (I-V) characteristics of GO films were recorded within a voltage range of −5 to +5 V using a Keithley picoamperemeter (model 6485) in a two-contact configuration. For each sample, three sets of measurements were performed using a different pair of contacts fabricated from silver alloy each time; the typical single contact surface area and inter-contact distance were 0.5 mm2 and 1.5 mm, respectively. Temperature-dependent resistance measurements were performed in a cryogenic insert with an exchange gas in the temperature range of 80–300 K. The resistance was measured in a two-contact configuration using a Keysight B2912A precision sourcemeter in constant voltage mode. Unless otherwise stated, a voltage of 1 V was applied. The samples have a rectangular shape with a typical size of
∼
0.5 mm × 0.5 mm. Raman spectra were measured using a Nanofinder 30 (SOL Instruments) spectrometer equipped with a 473 nm laser source and a 100
×
objective lens. The laser power at the sample was kept below 100 μW to avoid structural modification due to laser heating. For each sample, at least 12 spectra with acquisition time of 60 s were recorded at points randomly selected over the whole specimen area. The resulting spectra were despiked, background corrected, and deconvoluted using a Lorentzian lineshape for the D-band and an asymmetric Breit-Wigner-Fano line for the G-band [31]. This model is too simple to perfectly fit GO spectra, but it is rigid enough to provide stability to the fitting parameters and thus a meaningful interpretation. The deconvolution procedure yielded the following spectral parameters: disorder parameter defined as the D-to G-band intensity ratio (
I
D
/
I
G
), peak positions (
ω
D
and
ω
G
), and full widths at half maximum (FWHM(D) and FWHM(G)). To distinguish these parameters from those calculated from nondeconvoluted spectra for comparison (
I
D
/
I
G
,
ω
D
, and
ω
G
), the latter set are marked with ’(raw)’ notation. Note that
I
D
/
I
G
(raw) is higher than
I
D
/
I
G
since an asymmetric G-band contributes to the intensity in the D-band spectral region. When the focus was on a signal of sp-hybridized C, the spectra were recorded while constantly scanning the laser beam over the sample surface. In this case, the laser power was set to 100 μW, and typically 72 spectra with an acquisition time of 2 s were collected over a scan area of 1800 μm2 at a scan speed of 2.5 μm/s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of ion irradiation in the electronic stopping regime
ce:section.ce:para:
Both virgin and irradiated samples were investigated using XPS. The main objective of the XPS measurements was to examine the evolution of the C/O ratio and survival of particular oxygen-containing groups upon ion irradiation. The oxidation degree of the virgin films (C/O ratio of 2.53
±
0.07) was lower than that of fully oxidized GO (C/O = 2 [1,32]).

The variation in the atomic fraction of C as a function of the ion fluence for Xe, Kr, and Ar ions is shown in Fig. 1 a. In all cases, the carbon atomic fraction increases nonlinearly with the ion dose, reaching a value of 0.882
±
0.003 for Kr-ion irradiation at a fluence of 3
⋅
1013 cm−2. This suggests a significant degree of deoxygenation and formation of rGO at high fluences. The data are reasonably well described by the direct impact model (according to Poisson law), providing preliminary evidence for the highly localized character of SHI-induced defunctionalization. Assuming cylindrical symmetry of the produced deoxygenated regions, their average diameters were estimated to be approximately 5.7, 4.8 and 4.2 nm for the 167 MeV Xe, 107 MeV Kr, and 46 MeV Ar ions, respectively. Irradiation with all the investigated SHIs led to a similar extent of deoxygenation with respect to the deposited electronic energy density (Fig. 1b). This indicates that the threshold electronic stopping power (
S
e
) value for GO defunctionalization is small (below 5 keV/nm), consistent with the fact that the majority of oxygen-containing groups decompose at temperatures as low as 100 °C. To study in detail the contributions of particular oxygen functionalities, we analyzed the XPS C1s and O1s core-level spectra (Fig. S1, Supp. Info.). A deconvolution procedure was performed using a Shirley background and five peaks of a mixed Gaussian-Lorentzian lineshape centered at 284.6, 286.7, 288.3, 289.7, and 291.0 eV, which were attributed to sp2 and sp3-hybridized carbon (C-C), carbon single bonded to oxygen in epoxide and hydroxyl groups (C-O), carbon double bonded to oxygen in carbonyl groups (C=O), carbon in carboxylic groups (O-C=O), and a shake-up satellite (π-
π
*
), respectively [33]. The dominant components in the virgin specimen were due to C-C and C-O carbons. After irradiation, a significant reduction in the intensity of the signals due to epoxide and hydroxyl groups was observed. The residual oxygen was mainly incorporated into double-bonded functionalities, which are known to be more thermally stable. The higher survival probability of these groups is especially noticeable for the ions with a low
S
e
value (46 MeV Ar), as evidenced by the C1s and O1s core-level spectra of Ar (
Φ
= 2.2
⋅
1013 cm−2) and Kr (
Φ
= 6
⋅
1013 cm−2) ion-irradiated specimens exhibiting essentially the same C/O ratio (Fig. S1 b, Supp. Info.).

The formation of nanosized rGO spots by SHI irradiation of GO was directly proved by AFM imaging in contact mode. Topography images of GO specimens irradiated with low doses of 107 MeV Kr ions (Fig. 2 a) reveal the presence of nanosized protrusions (observed as brighter spots) at the surface. Their diameters measured at the base were in the range of 22–30 nm (FWHMs of 12–17 nm), whereas their heights vary from 1 to 2.5 nm (Fig. 2b-c). The area number density of these nanohillocks increased with the dose, and at sufficiently high doses, they start to overlap (Fig. 2a). In addition to the investigation of the surface relief, measurements in lateral force (friction force) mode were conducted (Fig. 2 a, bottom). These measurements enable the differentiation of regions with different chemical compositions: in particular, they provide distinct contrast between unoxidized and oxidized regions on a graphene surface. Since the friction is lower on the unoxidized graphene surface, the oxygen-deficient regions appear as darker spots in the lateral force images. Thus, the ion beam-formed nanosized rGO spots can be easily recognized in Fig. 2 a, bottom. Their sizes are smaller (FWHMs
∼
6–9 nm) than those of protrusions and are in better agreement with the values obtained from direct impact-based calculations. Similar observations can be made in SEM images of 167 MeV Xe ion-irradiated GO films (Fig. 2d-e and 3 a-b). In this case, the protrusions are represented by 27–35 nm-diameter spots of higher SE signal intensity. Darker spots (7.5–10 nm in diameter) may represent graphitized regions (rGO), which are known to give lower signal intensity [15,17,27], but the presence of nanoholes or craters also cannot be excluded.

The impact of ion irradiation on the electrical properties of GO films was investigated for 46 MeV Ar ion-irradiated samples (Fig. 4 ). The in-plane conductivity measured at a voltage of 5 V gradually increased with irradiation dose and, at the highest dose, was almost three orders of magnitude higher than that of virgin GO. The I-V curves recorded at room temperature are linear and symmetric, which indicates that as the Ar ion dose increases, the GO transport behavior changes from insulating to ohmic. Notably, the surface atomic fraction of C atoms (Fig. 1a) and the sample conductivity show very similar trends with ion dose. To gain insight into the transport behavior changes, we conducted temperature-dependent resistance measurements. In the literature, the transport properties of GO and its derivatives are often described by variable range hopping (VRH), with a generalized equation:
R
( T )
=
R
0
+ exp
(
T
0
/ T )
p
, where
T
0
is a characteristic temperature related to the degree of disorder, and p is a characteristic exponent. The value of p distinguishes the conduction mechanism. In Mott formalism, considering a constant density of states around the Fermi level (
E
F
), p is related to the dimensionality D of the system
p = 1 /
(
D + 1
)
. In contrast, the Efros-Shklarskii VRH (ES-VRH) model, in which Coulomb interactions are important, assumes
p = 1 / 2
independently of the dimensionality. Thus far, different values of p have been reported for GO and its reduced-derivatives, including
p = 1 / 2
(ES-VRH) [34,35],
p = 1 / 3
(2D Mott-VRH) [36–38], and
p = 1 / 4
(3D Mott-VRH, for thick rGO films) [39].

Since the conductivity data can frequently be fitted relatively well by multiple models, special care is required when assigning a particular transport mechanism. Thus, to elucidate the conduction mechanism from the collected data, we employed resistance curve derivative analysis (RCDA) [35,40]. In the RCDA method, the value of the exponent p is obtained from the negative of the slope of the plot of
ln W
versus
ln T
, where W is the reduced activation energy, defined as
W = − ∂ ln ρ
(
T
)
/ ∂ ln T
. In our case, the p values determined for the whole temperature range were in the range
0.34 − 0.43
, i.e., they were between the valuse characteristic of the 2D Mott-VRH and ES-VRH mechanisms. The only exception was the virgin sample with a p value of 0.86, but in this case, due to the high increase in resistivity, only the high-temperature portion (i.e., 200–300 K) of the
R
(
T
)
dependence was recorded. This scenario suggests that
R
(
T
)
cannot be accurately described using a single conduction mechanism. The much higher p value for virgin sample suggests a contribution at higher temperatures of a thermally activated (TA) process (
p = 1
), expressed by
R
(
T
)
=
R
0
+ exp
(
E
a
/
k
B
T
)
, where
E
a
is the activation energy, and
k
B
is the Boltzmann constant. Indeed, for all samples, the good linearity of this part of the
R
(
T
)
dependency is visible once the data are plotted in Arrhenius coordinates (
ln R
vs.
T
− 1
, Fig. 4c). The p values determined after omitting the high-temperature
R
(
T
)
region were very close to 0.5 (0.48–0.51), as expected for ES-VRH conduction. Thus, we assume that the conduction mechanism involves a combination of a thermally activated process and EF-VRH. This explanation is in contrast to the work of Eda et al. [37] and Negishi et al. [38], who observed a transition from Arrhenius to 2D Mott-VRH. The authors, however, investigated fully reduced GO specimens [37] or, as in Ref. [38], applied a special ethanol vapor treatment to provide better recovery of the graphene lattice. In our study, we investigated locally reduced GO samples. For such samples, as well as for other samples with structure and size-induced disorders, the density of states near
E
F
is no longer constant, and at sufficiently low temperatures, it vanishes linearly with energy. For this reason, the temperature dependence of the resistance is better described by the ES-VRH (
p = 1 / 2
) model. Furthermore, this model highlights the localization of wavefunctions inside recovered graphene domains [34] and thus better suits the expected QD nature of the fabricated structures. The resulting fitting parameters were used to estimate the localization length
ξ = 2.8
e
2
/
( 4 π ε
ε
0
k
B
T
0
)
and band gap (
E
g
∼ ℏ
υ
F
/ ξ
) [35]. Similarly, as in Ref. [35], we assumed a graphene Fermi velocity
υ
F
of 106 m/s and the relative dielectric constant ε value of 3.5, as calculated from the refractive index and extinction coefficient values obtained from the ellipsometry measurements of chemically reduced GO film [41]. For 46 MeV Ar ion-irradiated specimens, both the activation energy (TA) and the characteristic temperature (ES-VRH) decrease with the irradiation dose (Fig. 4 d and Table 2 ). Since the activation energies are rather small, the Arrhenius behavior represents thermally activated hopping (i.e., nearest-neighbor hopping) rather than band-like conductivity. A decrease in the characteristic temperature
T
0
indicates an increase in the localization length. Such a trend is expected from the increase in the number density of the rGO spots formed. The calculated
E
g
decreases from 2.3 eV for a dose of
6 ⋅
10
11
cm−2 to ca. 1.4 eV, for a dose of
6 ⋅
10
12
cm−2, showing that the material changes from an insulator to a semiconductor. Although
E
g
values should be considered approximate estimates they fall within an interval defined by severely oxidized graphene (
E
g
2.8–3.6 eV [42–44]) and well-reduced graphene (
E
g
∼
1 eV [43]). The slightly worse characteristics obtained for the sample irradiated with the highest dose can be related to the damage to the recovered structures at high doses. All the above results indicate that the obtained nanopatterned structure can be considered as array of highly conductive QD-like spots embedded in a non-conducting matrix.

The structural changes in the irradiated GO films were investigated by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 1c-d and Fig. S2, Supp. Info.). It is important to note that the spectral evolution reflects the net effects of irradiation-induced lattice damaging and its restoration due to defunctionalization. In the case of 167 MeV Xe ion irradiation, the disorder parameter is approximately constant up to a dose of 1012 cm−2 and then decreases at higher fluences. Initially, the decrease in
I
D
/
I
G
may be interpreted as an indicator of structural ordering; however, a more thorough analysis reveals a significant broadening of the spectral features upon irradiation. This behavior suggests that (i) the structure of the initial GO falls within stage II in the amorphization trajectory proposed by Ferrari and Robertson (i.e., with an interdefect length lower than 3 nm and some sp3 C content) [45], and (ii) at high fluences, we move forward on the amorphization trajectory toward an increasingly amorphized structure. For Ar ion irradiation, a slight ordering of the structure at low irradiation doses and a lower defect creation efficiency relative to the deposited electronic energy density were observed. The former effect is evidenced by an increase in the
I
D
/
I
G
ratio accompanied by D- and G-peak narrowing. Both phenomena can be explained by the lower electronic stopping power (ca. 5.6 keV/nm) characteristic of the interaction of Ar ions with sp2 carbon. This value is lower than the threshold value for damage creation in graphite (6–8 keV/nm [46–48]) but slightly higher than those predicted for graphene (3.5–4.2 keV/nm [49]) and carbon nanotubes (
≤
5 keV/nm [50]). Thus, in this case, the damage creation is significantly suppressed, and structural ordering due to functional group removal is more likely to occur. Notably, even for thermal reduction, GO lattice restoration is not very pronounced. The reason for this remains unclear, although it was suggested that the lower than expected lattice healing arises from the evolution of gaseous decomposition products. For the 107 MeV Kr ions (S e = 11.4 keV/nm), we carried out high-dose irradiations only, showing that amorphization efficiency is intermediate between those of Xe and Ar ions. A weak ordering effect was observed for GO samples of a slightly lower initial degree of oxidation irradiated over a broader dose range (Fig. S3 in the Supp. Info.).

A noticeable phenomenon for GO films irradiated at high doses (
≥
10
12
cm−2) of 167 MeV Xe and 107 MeV Kr ions was the appearance of a broad band located at 2066-2260 cm−1 and centered ca. 2160 cm−1. Signals in this range are the fingerprints of the presence of sp-hybridized carbon. The other two possible candidates should be rejected for the following reasons: (i) the G∗ band appears at higher wavenumbers (2450-2460 cm−1); additionally, it exhibits slightly dispersive behavior and is absent in the Raman spectra of highly disordered systems (e.g., irradiated graphene); (ii) the vibration of C
≡
N group is characterized by a low scattering cross-section and is typically visible in UV-excited spectra of highly nitrogenated carbons (
>
20% of N) [51], whereas the N content in our samples is ca. 1.5%. Notably, in agreement with [52], sp-C structures were unstable under laser radiation during Raman measurements, and even for short exposure times, significant sp-signal degradation occurred. For this reason, Raman spectra of exemplary samples were recorded while constantly scanning the laser beam over the sample surface. These spectra showed not only much higher sp-C signal intensity than those recorded under ordinary conditions but also reveled the presence of a less-intensive shoulder ranging down to 1930 cm−1 (maximum ca. 2018 cm−1) (Fig. 5 a). The structures represented by this new signal were especially sensitive to air atmosphere – their detection was possible only for samples measured immediately after irradiation. In contrast, the sp-C structures providing a signal at 2160 cm−1 were pretty stable upon exposure to air – only a minor reduction in signal intensity occurred after storing the samples for one week in air.

Chemical studies have shown that sp-hybridized species are extremely reactive unless their chains are isolated and protected by different end groups [54–58]. Accordingly, in our case, a mixed sp-sp2 cluster system consisting of carbon chains terminated on both ends with a graphene domain most likely formed. Based on the position of the 2160 cm−1 band, it can be concluded that the chains are mainly polyynes of relatively short length. A rough calculation, according to the Kastner formula [59], provides an average C atom number in the sp-chains of 9.7; based on experimental frequencies, a value of 8 can be predicted [60]. The spectral range of 1930–2066 cm−1 is characteristic of both polycumulenes and long-chain polyynic species (
>
C12). However, its assignment to polycumulenes seems to be more plausible due to its rapid disappearance rate and even greater difficulty in observing polycumulenes ex situ [54,57]. Given that sp-C structures are formed only at highest electronic stopping powers (167 MeV Xe and 107 MeV Kr ion-irradiation), these carbon chains are very likely bridges joining two opposite sides of a nanohole created inside the track core. Confirmation of this assumption can be delivered by a molecular dynamics study of SHI-irradiated graphene [49]. Although the authors did not comment on this aspect, similar carbon-chain molecular junctions are clearly seen in the snapshots from their simulations. Interestingly, a similar carbon chain length can be found in the simulation images for irradiation with 84 MeV Ta, corresponding to a similar electronic stopping power (14.7 keV/nm). One of the possible mechanisms of sp-C chain formation is the breaking of cross-bonds [61] and/or removal of C atoms at both sides of the zigzag chain. Preliminary DFT calculations (B3LYP functional with 6–31G(d) basis sets) for such structures were performed, assuming the damaged graphene region to be flat. Due to geometrical constraints, the frequencies of the longitudinal optical (LO) Raman mode (
ω
α
) are blue-shifted and bond length alternation (BLA) is decreased in comparison with those of fully optimized, phenyl end-capped polyyne sp chains of the same length (Fig. 5b-c).

Taking the thermal instability of sp-C structures into account, each ion impact is likely to create new sp-C chains inside the track core and disintegrate previously created chains that are close enough to the heated zone surrounding ion trajectory. Thus, the concentration of sp-C is expected to build up until a certain saturation level at high doses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of ion irradiation in the nuclear stopping regime
ce:section.ce:para: To prove that the phenomena reported above, i.e., the localized reduction of GO and formation of sp-hybridized carbon chains, are characteristic of irradiation in the electronic stopping regime, we performed a comparative irradiation study with keV energy range ions. Such ions predominantly lose their energy in elastic collisions with target nuclei.

First, AFM imaging of 200 keV Xe ion-irradiated GO (Fig. S4, Supp. Info.) revealed lack of the nanometer-sized deoxygenated regions observed in SHI-irradiated samples. Even for high-dose irradiation (Fig. S4b-c, Supp. Info.), the sample surface was pretty smooth. Similarly, no noticeable change in sample morphology was found by SEM imaging (Fig. 3c). The preservation of a sheet-like structure is in agreement with [15] but in contrast to studies by Refs. [16] and [14]. These authors reported the formation of nanocones and whirlpool-like structures at high fluences; however the highest fluence used in their study was at least an order of magnitude higher than those used in our study.

Raman spectra of GO specimens irradiated with different doses of 200 keV Xe ions are shown in Fig. S5 a (Supp. Info.). Analysis of the deconvoluted Raman spectral data (Fig. S5 b, Supp. Info.) shows that with increasing ion fluence, the FWHMs of the D- and G-band increase, the position the G-band significantly downshifts, the position of the D-band moderately upshifts, and the ID/IG ratio progressively decreases. Very little or no ordering of the structure can be observed at low ion fluences. These results prove that, with increasing ion dose, the sample progressively amorphizes and, at the highest dose, its composition is close to that of tetrahedral amorphous carbon. Note that for the two highest doses, there is a slight increase in the
I
D
/
I
G
(raw) ratio determined from the nondeconvoluted spectra, but the effect is predicted to result from shifting and broadening of the spectral bands rather than from structural ordering. The major mechanism of radiation damage in this energy range is due to knock-on atom displacements initiated by primary ballistic collisions of the ions with GO lattice. These displacements result in the formation of vacancies, including mono-, di-, and multivacancies. The displaced atoms can be sputtered from the surface or inserted either into a lattice (adatoms) or between the graphitic layers (interstitials). Since at room temperature, the mobilities of both vacancies and carbon adatoms are low, the defect creation rate is higher than the defect annihilation rate. Under continuous irradiation, this difference in rates leads to defect accumulation and finally to the formation of amorphous structure [62].

Even though the maximum dose of 200 keV Xe ions was much higher than those of 167 MeV Xe and 107 MeV Kr ions and the GO was much more amorphized, no sp-hybridized C structures were detected. Importantly, no sp-C signal was detected for 46 MeV Ar ion-irradiated samples, although both the fluence range and the density of electronic energy deposited in the system for Ar ion irradiation were higher than those for 107 MeV Kr and 167 MeV Xe irradiations. As mentioned above, the electronic stopping power in this case was lower and below the threshold value for defect formation in graphite. These two cases (48 Me Ar and 150 keV Xe) strongly imply that sp-C structures are formed exclusively due to high electronic energy losses.

Changes in the surface elemental composition are shown Fig. S6 a (Supp. Info.). The C/O ratio increases with the increase in the ion dose, and at the highest dose, the C content reaches 90.5 at.%. In this case, fitting the direct impact model to the data gave poor fit quality. Presumably, the deoxygenation process is complex and cannot be explained by a single mechanism [16]. Preferential removal (sputtering) of oxygen has already been reported for a variety of oxides ([63] and references therein) and polymers [64,65], typically accompanied by graphitization in the former case. In our experiments, the ion flux was kept low to avoid global heating of the GO film, but local lattice heating to extremely high temperatures, i.e., the thermal spike, is still expected to occur during the final evolution stage within collision cascades [66]. Another important reason for the deoxygenation is the lower binding energy of O atoms compared to that of C atoms. Specifically, the CC bond energy in graphite is 4.93 eV [67], and since mobilizing an internal C atom would require the rupture of several bonds, the displacement energy is estimated to be 19–25 eV [68–70]. For comparison, the binding energies of oxygen in separated epoxy and hydroxyl groups are 2.1 and 0.7 eV, respectively [71]. This difference is enough for less-energetic species to preferentially knock-out O (and probably H [72]) atoms. The other effects that may possibly contribute to the preferential removal of O atoms are discussed in Ref. [16].

Finally, we focus on the electrical properties of the irradiated samples. In the reported papers on GO reduction, improvement in electrical conductivity is typically well correlated with changes in surface elemental composition and structural information obtained from Raman spectra. For example, in the case of 30 keV Ga ion irradiation, both the sample conductivity and carbon content initially strongly increase with the ion fluence, but once the material start to amorphize, a gradual decrease in conductivity is observed [15]. In a final irradiation stage, due to the increased amount of structural defects, the sample conductivity was as low as that of virgin GO. A similar quantitative correlation between changes in carbon content and conductivity is visible for our previously discussed 46 MeV Ar ion-irradiation experiments. In contrast to the abovementioned results, the conductivity evolution of 200 keV Xe ion-irradiated GO followed a quite different pathway (Fig. S6 b, Supp. Info.). For doses up to 1014 cm−2, there was only a minor increase in conductivity, although the increase in C content was rather high. The most significant conductivity improvement occurred upon high-dose irradiation (1015-1016 cm−2), even though these samples possessed the highest amorphization level. This discrepancy can possibly be explained considering the ion ranges and atomic displacement densities produced per ion as a function of target depth. According to the SRIM-2013 simulation (fully oxidized GO C/O/H 2/1/0.5, assumed density of 1.36 g/cm3 [73]), for 200 keV Xe ions, the defunctionalization is expected to proceed most efficiently at a depth close to the ion range (
∼
100 nm). The number of displaced O and H atoms at the surface layer is ca. 3 times lower. Therefore, it is very probable that intermediate-dose irradiation leads to the formation of a conductive layer located below the sample surface but charge careers have limited access to that layer. This is because conductive regions are separated by dielectric regions – barriers with dimensions influenced by irradiation conditions. This effect is harder to achieve for 30 keV Ga ions since the ion range is shorter (
∼
30 nm) and the number of displacement events per unit volume for the bulk and surface layer is only 2 times higher. Interestingly, the I-V curves recorded for GO irradiated with intermediate doses (1014-1015 cm−2) of 200 keV Xe ions are the only ones that show remarkable deviations from linearity (not shown). With the application of an electric field, the effective barrier height can be lowered, and starting from a particular voltage, previously isolated graphene regions contribute to the conductivity. The lack of a hysteresis loop in the obtained I-V curves suggests that no charge-carrier capture occurs in rGO regions and in their borders with barriers. These assumptions require more detailed investigation, and thus, our future works related to locally reduced GO systems will focus on resolving the border structure of the rGO and GO matrix by frequency- and temperature-dependent conductivity measurements.
We demonstrated a fabrication method of nanosized rGO spots by SHI bombardment.

Formation of conductive nanosized rGO spots was confirmed indirectly by a global conductivity increase in the films and directly by AFM imaging in lateral force mode. Both the area number density and the diameter of the rGO spots can be tailored by a suitable choice of irradiation parameters (i.e., ion type, its energy, and fluence). The degree of deoxygenation determined for SHIs of different energies was found to scale well with the deposited electronic energy density, suggesting that the
S
e
threshold for defunctionalization is very low. This is in accordance with the low binding energy of the most of the oxygen-containing functionalities attached to GO and supported by the fact that a similar SHI-induced chemical transformation (graphitization) occurs very easily for a variety of oxygen-bearing polymers [30]. In contrast, no such scaling with deposited energy density was found for changes in the disorder parameter (
I
D
/
I
G
). In this case, ions with a low
S
e
value introduce less structural damage than expected from the deposited energy density and are more likely to lead to structural ordering at intermediate doses (i.e., in the track non-overlapping regime). For ions with a high
S
e
value, defect creation proceeds more efficiently and is accompanied by formation of sp-hybridized carbon chains. Based on their relatively high ex situ stability and Raman spectral features, the structures that survived air exposure are polyynic carbon chains with an average C atom number of ca. 8–10, terminated at both ends with graphene domains. Accordingly, it is very probable that sp-C chains are bridges joining two sides of a nanohole (antidot) created inside the track core. Creation of nanoholes in graphene by SHI irradiation was recently predicted theoretically [49] and confirmed experimentally [74,75].

We assume that SHIs with low to intermediate
S
e
values would create mainly rGO spots that can be considered graphene QDs embedded in a non-conducting matrix, whereas ions with high
S
e
values can form coupled QDs-antidots with sp-C bridges. Both systems are predicted to have interesting transport properties in high magnetic fields [76,77] and will be the subject of our future investigations.

Finally, we would like to stress that the phenomena reported above, i.e., the localized reduction of GO and formation of sp-hybridized carbon chains, occurred only in the electronic stopping regime and were not observed for keV energy range ions in either our irradiation experiments or the literature. In this case, however, irradiation under appropriately selected conditions can lead to the formation of an undersurface layer of higher conductivity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Dr. N. E. Lizunov (FLNR, JINR) for performing the SEM experiments and to Dr. A. P. Orlov (IRE RAS) for his helpful suggestions and assistance with setting up the temperature-dependent resistance measurements. This research was partially supported by the Cooperation Program between Polish scientific institutions and JINR within Theme No. 04-5-1131-2017/2021. NAN gratefully acknowledges financial support by the Russian Foundation for Basic Research (RFBR, No. 18-32-00449) and the Russian President's scholarship, project No. SP-5416.2018.2. AVF (themperature-dependent resistance measurements) acknowledges support by the Russian Science Foundation (project No. 16-12-10411).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.09.042.
